title: Serialization constructors should be secured
description: Because serialization constructors allocate and initialize objects, security checks that are present on regular constructors must also be present on a serialization constructor. Failure to do so would allow callers that could not otherwise create an instance to use the serialization constructor to do this. This rule raises an issue when a type implements the System.Runtime.Serialization.ISerializable interface, is not a delegate or interface, is declared in an assembly that allows partially trusted callers and has a constructor that takes a System.Runtime.Serialization.SerializationInfo object and a System.Runtime.Serialization.StreamingContext object which is not secured by a security check, but one or more of the regular constructors in the type is secured.
recommendation: Remove the problematic code and refactor as needed.

logic.detection_conditions: ISerializable class with secured regular constructor but unsecured serialization constructor

conditions: At least one regular constructor has [SecurityPermission] attribute
Serialization constructor (SerializationInfo, StreamingContext) lacks security attribute

exclusion_conditions: Classes where no constructors have security attributes
Serialization constructors that have matching security attributes
never_trigger_on: Classes where no constructor has SecurityPermission
logic.reasoning: Serialization constructors can bypass regular constructor security checks. If regular constructors have security demands, serialization constructors must have equivalent protection. Uses custom function to correlate ISerializable implementation, find secured constructors, and verify serialization constructor security.
